title: The Frontier of Modern History
body:
Modern history is increasingly shaped by digital archives, machine-readable records, and new ways of comparing large historical datasets. Historians can now search, tag, and cross-reference at a scale that was impossible before.

Oral history and public history are also gaining prominence because they widen whose stories are preserved. That shifts the field away from elite-only narratives toward more complete accounts.

Scientific tools such as ancient DNA, isotope analysis, and climate reconstruction are helping historians place migration, disease, and environmental change into the historical record.

The frontier is therefore both more technical and more democratic than before.
keyPoints: DNA and climate data enrich the record
Public history is changing who gets remembered
Interpretation is moving beyond elite narratives
furtherReading: The History Manifesto by Jo Guldi and David Armitage
